14.1.title: परं ज्ञानम् — superlative knowledge announced
14.1.body: Krishna opens with प्रवक्ष्यामि (√वच्, लृट्, उत्तम-पुरुष-एकवचन) — "I shall declare" — staking a future commitment. The phrase ज्ञानानां ज्ञानमुत्तमम् is षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष piled on superlative: "of all knowledges, the highest knowledge" — and इतः ("from this") signals that the route to परा सिद्धि passes through this very teaching.

14.1.title: मुनयः गताः — the sages' transcendence as proof
14.1.body: गताः is a कृदन्त (past participle of √गम्) functioning predicatively with मुनयः सर्वे — "all the sages have gone." Krishna grounds the new teaching not in mere assertion but in completed precedent: the यत्-ज्ञात्वा ("having known which") absolutive frames the guṇa-analysis as the historical liberation-key, not a fresh experiment.

14.2.title: साधर्म्यम् — likeness, not identity
14.2.body: मम साधर्म्यम् ("likeness with Me") is carefully chosen: not सायुज्य (merger) but साधर्म्य (sharing My nature). The compound उपाश्रित्य (absolutive, "having taken refuge in") makes ज्ञानम् the support; सर्गे and प्रलये (both सप्तमी) locate the freedom precisely at the cosmic seams where ordinary beings are born and destroyed.

14.2.title: नोपजायन्ते न व्यथन्ति — twin negations of saṃsāra
14.2.body: Two finite verbs in लट् (उपजायन्ते and व्यथन्ति) are jointly negated by न ... न च — "neither are they born nor do they suffer." The verse defines liberation by what does not happen: the saṃsāra-clock (sarga, pralaya) keeps ticking, but the jñānin stands outside its swing.

14.3.title: महद्ब्रह्म as womb — cosmogenesis in two words
14.3.body: मम योनिः महद्ब्रह्म collapses Sāṅkhya cosmology into a single equation: prakṛti (here called महद्ब्रह्म) is Krishna's womb. दधामि (√धा, लट्, उत्तम-एकवचन) — "I place" — and the locative तस्मिन् make Krishna the seed-depositor; the embryo (गर्भम्, द्वितीया) is the puruṣa-contact that triggers manifestation.

14.3.title: सम्भवः सर्वभूतानाम् — universal genesis
14.3.body: सम्भवः (nominative subject) and the षष्ठी-बहुवचन सर्वभूतानाम् together mean "the origination of all beings" — and ततः ("from that") anaphorically links back to the womb-seed pair. भवति (लट्) keeps the cosmogonic act in the eternal present: this is not a one-time event but the ongoing mechanism.

14.4.title: बीजप्रदः पिता — the seed-giving father
14.4.body: बीजप्रदः is a कृदन्त-based तत्पुरुष ("seed-giver"), apposed to पिता and equated with अहम् — Krishna casts Himself in the paternal role, while ब्रह्म महत् is the योनि (mother). The dual-parent imagery resolves the apparent Sāṅkhya dualism: both prakṛti and puruṣa converge in Krishna.

14.4.title: सर्वयोनिषु — every species under one principle
14.4.body: सर्वयोनिषु (सप्तमी-बहुवचन) — "in all wombs" — universalises the claim across species. The relative-correlative यः ... तासाम् ties मूर्तयः (forms, प्रथमा-बहुवचन) to a single generative source: every visible form across every species traces back to the same womb-and-seed pair.

14.5.title: निबध्नन्ति देहिनम् — the three binders introduced
14.5.body: निबध्नन्ति (√बन्ध् + नि, लट्, प्रथम-बहुवचन) is the operative verb: सत्त्वं रजः तमः, all प्रथमा-एकवचन, are the bound triad-subject; देहिनम् (द्वितीया, "the embodied one") is the object. The adjective अव्ययम् ("unchanging") on देहिनम् is the irony of the chapter — the binders bind something that is intrinsically unbindable.

14.5.title: प्रकृतिसम्भवाः — guṇas as prakṛti's exhalation
14.5.body: प्रकृतिसम्भवाः is a बहुव्रीहि ("those whose origin is prakṛti") qualifying गुणाः — the guṇas are not independent realities but prakṛti's three modes. देहे (सप्तमी, "in the body") locates the binding site: the body is the staging-ground where prakṛti's threads tie down the witness.

14.6.title: सत्त्वम् — luminosity as a subtle chain
14.6.body: Three adjectives stack on सत्त्वम्: निर्मलत्वात् (पञ्चमी, "because of its purity"), प्रकाशकम् (कृदन्त, "illuminating"), अनामयम् ("free from disease"). The verb बध्नाति (लट्, प्रथम-एकवचन) takes two तृतीया-instruments: सुखसङ्गेन and ज्ञानसङ्गेन — even sukha and jñāna become fetters when clung to.

14.6.title: सङ्गेन — attachment, not the object, is the bond
14.6.body: Krishna deploys सङ्ग as the binding mechanism three times in three verses (सुखसङ्ग, कर्मसङ्ग, the implicit मोहसङ्ग of tamas). The तृतीया instrumental case is precise: sukha and jñāna do not bind by themselves — only when grasped via सङ्ग. Pure sattva remains an obstacle so long as it is held.

14.7.title: रजः रागात्मकम् — passion as the self of rajas
14.7.body: रागात्मकम् is a बहुव्रीहि ("whose ātman is rāga"), making rajas not merely passion-producing but passion-constituted. तृष्णासङ्गसमुद्भवम् is a long तत्पुरुष — "arising from thirst-attachment" — and विद्धि (लोट्, मध्यम-एकवचन, "know!") is Krishna's imperative urging analytic recognition of this internal mechanism.

14.7.title: कर्मसङ्गेन निबध्नाति — work as rajas's leash
14.7.body: Where sattva bound through ज्ञानसङ्ग, rajas binds through कर्मसङ्ग (तृतीया) — attachment to action. देहिनम् (द्वितीया) is again the bound object. The parallel structure across 6, 7, 8 is deliberate: same verb (निबध्नाति), same victim, different instrumental — three distinct ropes, one common knot.

14.8.title: तमः अज्ञानजम् — born of ignorance, born for ignorance
14.8.body: अज्ञानजम् ("born from ignorance") is a तत्पुरुष कृदन्त adjective; मोहनम् ("deluding") is another कृदन्त governing सर्वदेहिनाम् (षष्ठी, "of all embodied beings"). Tamas alone is universalised — sattva and rajas vary by person, but tamas is everyone's baseline drag.

14.8.title: प्रमादालस्यनिद्राभिः — the three-fold rope of dullness
14.8.body: The instrumental compound प्रमाद-आलस्य-निद्राभिः (तृतीया-बहुवचन of a द्वन्द्व) names tamas's three operational tools: heedlessness, sloth, sleep. The verb निबध्नाति repeats from 6 and 7, but the instrumentals have darkened: not sukha or karma but the inability to even register what is happening.

14.9.title: सञ्जयति — each guṇa's signature attachment
14.9.body: सञ्जयति (causative of √सञ्ज्, लट्) means "attaches firmly" — sattva attaches to sukha (सप्तमी), rajas to karma (सप्तमी), tamas to pramāda (सप्तमी). The locatives mark the attachment-target. The absolutive आवृत्य ("having covered") shows tamas's distinct mechanism: it first occludes ज्ञानम् (द्वितीया) and only then drags into heedlessness.

14.9.title: ज्ञानमावृत्य — tamas's two-step takeover
14.9.body: Where sattva and rajas need only one verb each, tamas needs two: ज्ञानम् आवृत्य (absolutive, "having veiled knowledge") and then सञ्जयति. The उत at verse-end emphasises the sequence — first the lights go out, then the falling begins. Knowledge is the lamp; tamas's first move is always to blow it out.

14.10.title: अभिभूय — guṇa-dominance as suppression
14.10.body: अभिभूय (absolutive, "having overpowered") takes रजः and तमः as द्वितीया objects, and सत्त्वम् (प्रथमा) becomes the surviving subject of भवति. The verse maps a rotating hierarchy: at any moment one guṇa is on top, the other two suppressed. The triadic permutation in line 2 (रजः सत्त्वं तमः... तमः सत्त्वं रजः) makes the rotation explicit.

14.10.title: The internal weather of the mind
14.10.body: This is the chapter's most psychologically immediate verse: the guṇas are not fixed identities but shifting dominances. The repeated भवति (लट्) describes a present-tense reality — moment by moment, one mode rises and the other two recede. Self-observation is the skill: noticing which guṇa is currently abhibhū-ing the others.

14.11.title: सर्वद्वारेषु प्रकाशः — sattva's diagnostic sign
14.11.body: सर्वद्वारेषु (सप्तमी-बहुवचन, "in all gateways") refers to the indriyas — the doors of the body. प्रकाशः उपजायते ("radiance arises") and ज्ञानम् as a parallel subject diagnose sattva-dominance. विद्यात् (विधिलिङ्, प्रथम-एकवचन, "one should know") gives the inferential rule: from the symptom (lucidity in every sense-door) deduce the cause (विवृद्धं सत्त्वम्).

14.11.title: विद्यात् — inference from inside
14.11.body: The विधिलिङ् mood (विद्यात्) is not a command but an instruction to the practitioner: when these signs appear, *infer* sattva's increase. Krishna is teaching diagnostic self-reading. The body itself becomes the readout: every sense-gate transparent, knowledge arising — this is sattva's signature trace.

14.12.title: लोभः प्रवृत्तिः आरम्भः — rajas's five-fold tell
14.12.body: Five nominatives in apposition — लोभः, प्रवृत्तिः, आरम्भः कर्मणाम्, अशमः, स्पृहा — are all subjects of the single verb जायन्ते. The षष्ठी कर्मणाम् qualifies आरम्भः specifically: "the launching of activities." Rajas reveals itself not as one symptom but as a cluster: greed, restlessness, project-fever, refusal to settle, craving.

14.12.title: विवृद्धे रजसि — locative absolute as diagnostic frame
14.12.body: रजसि विवृद्धे is a सप्तमी absolute ("when rajas has grown") — a temporal/causal frame. The construction parallels 14.11 and 14.13, building a three-verse diagnostic atlas: in each, locative absolute states the condition, finite verb names the symptoms. The structure itself is the teaching — guṇa-recognition is condition-driven.

14.13.title: अप्रकाशः अप्रवृत्तिः — tamas as four-fold symptom
14.13.body: Where rajas had five positive symptoms, tamas has four — and two begin with the negative prefix अ-: अप्रकाशः and अप्रवृत्तिः. Tamas literally shows up as the absence of what sattva and rajas produce: no light, no initiative — then प्रमादः and मोहः layer on heedlessness and delusion. The verb जायन्ते (लट्, बहुवचन) carries the same diagnostic form as 14.12.

14.13.title: The dark mirror of 14.11–12
14.13.body: Read together, 14.11, 14.12, 14.13 form a triptych — each opens with the symptoms, closes with the सप्तमी absolute (विवृद्धे sattva/rajas/tamas). The structural parallelism teaches as much as the content: the same diagnostic move applies to all three, and the chapter trains the practitioner to use this template on any internal state.

14.14.title: सत्त्वे प्रवृद्धे — death's guṇa-conditioned outcome
14.14.body: सत्त्वे प्रवृद्धे is a सप्तमी absolute ("when sattva is grown"); प्रलयं याति is a death-phrase where प्रलयम् (द्वितीया) is the destination of याति (लट्). The deceased (देहभृत् — "body-bearer") then प्रतिपद्यते (आत्मनेपद, "attains") अमलान् लोकान् of the उत्तमविदाम् — the worlds of those who know the highest.

14.14.title: अमलान् लोकान् — adjective tracking case
14.14.body: अमलान् (द्वितीया-बहुवचन-पुल्लिङ्ग) agrees in all three with लोकान् — classic विशेषण-विशेष्य. Sāmānādhikaraṇya here renders the destination unmistakable: not just any worlds, but stainless ones — sattva's purity at the deathbed transmits to the post-mortem geography.

14.15.title: रजसि / तमसि — twin descents in parallel structure
14.15.body: Two parallel constructions govern the verse: रजसि प्रलयं गत्वा (absolutive क्त्वान्त, "having gone to dissolution in rajas") and प्रलीनः तमसि (कृदन्त past participle, "dissolved in tamas"). The destinations rhyme syntactically: कर्मसङ्गिषु (सप्तमी, "among the karma-attached") and मूढयोनिषु (सप्तमी, "in deluded wombs"). Both जायते verbs (लट्, आत्मनेपद) keep the rebirth-mechanics tightly parallel to 14.14.

14.15.title: Posthumous geography as guṇa-output
14.15.body: Across 14.14–15 the rule is exact: dying sattvic → svarga-like clean worlds; dying rajasic → reborn among the karma-bonded (humans); dying tamasic → animal/insentient wombs. The verb जायते is identical for the last two; only the locative changes. The guṇa at death is the address-router.

14.16.title: फलम् — three guṇas, three fruits
14.16.body: आहुः (perfect, प्रथम-बहुवचन, "they say") sets the verse in traditional reportage. सुकृतस्य कर्मणः (षष्ठी qualifying phlam) — "of well-done action" — yields the सात्त्विकम् निर्मलं फलम्. The triplet that follows is compressed: रजसः फलं दुःखम् / तमसः फलम् अज्ञानम् — three parallel षष्ठी-नामिनी pairs in a single śloka.

14.16.title: Action's quality determines fruit's flavor
14.16.body: The verse is less about karmic mechanics than about flavor: the same activity, performed under different guṇas, produces qualitatively different outcomes. निर्मलम् for sattva, दुःखम् for rajas, अज्ञानम् for tamas — the fruit *tastes* like the guṇa that produced it. This is the practical bridge from 14.5–13 (binding) to 14.18 (trajectory).

14.17.title: सत्त्वात् — sources mapped to outputs
14.17.body: Each guṇa is in पञ्चमी (ablative of source): सत्त्वात्, रजसः, तमसः — "from sattva, from rajas, from tamas." The verbs are minimal: सञ्जायते (लट्) for jñāna and lobha, भवतः (dual, for प्रमादमोहौ) and the appositional एव च for अज्ञानम्. The triple ablative drives home that these are not co-occurrences but causal emanations.

14.17.title: अज्ञानम् as tamas's recursive fruit
14.17.body: Tamas yields प्रमाद, मोह, and अज्ञान — and note: 14.8 already said tamas is itself अज्ञानजम् (born of ignorance). The fruit is the seed: ignorance produces tamas which produces more ignorance. This recursive loop is why tamas is the hardest to escape — it cannot be diagnosed from within itself.

14.18.title: ऊर्ध्वं मध्ये अधः — the three-tier elevator
14.18.body: Three adverbs of direction (ऊर्ध्वम्, मध्ये, अधः) anchor three subject groups: सत्त्वस्थाः, राजसाः, तामसाः — all बहुवचन, all प्रथमा. The verbs गच्छन्ति (twice) and तिष्ठन्ति (for the middle) are also parallel. The compound जघन्यगुणवृत्तिस्थाः ("those standing in the conduct of the lowest guṇa") modifies तामसाः with diagnostic detail.

14.18.title: Verticality as cosmic destiny
14.18.body: The verse collapses 14.14–15 into a single vertical diagram: up, middle, down. The guṇa one inhabits determines not just rebirth quality but cosmic altitude. Krishna is summarising before pivoting — 14.19 will introduce the escape route, but only after the trap is fully mapped.

14.19.title: नान्यं गुणेभ्यः कर्तारम् — the witness's recognition
14.19.body: द्रष्टा ("the seer," प्रथमा-एकवचन) is the subject; अनुपश्यति (लट्, प्रथम-एकवचन) the verb. कर्तारम् (द्वितीया-एकवचन, "the doer") with negative न ... अन्यम् gives the doctrine: the seer sees no agent other than the guṇas. पञ्चमी गुणेभ्यः ("other than the guṇas") is the contrast-pivot.

14.19.title: मद्भावम् अधिगच्छति — the leap beyond
14.19.body: गुणेभ्यः परं वेत्ति ("and knows [Me] beyond the guṇas") adds the second clause; मद्भावम् ("My state") is the द्वितीया object of अधिगच्छति (लट्). Right-seeing has two halves: negatively, no agent but the guṇas; positively, what is *beyond* them is Me. Liberation begins as a correctly-bisected vision.

14.20.title: गुणानेतान् अतीत्य — the absolutive of transcendence
14.20.body: अतीत्य (absolutive, "having transcended") takes गुणान् त्रीन् (द्वितीया-बहुवचन) as object — three guṇas crossed in one stroke. देहसमुद्भवान् (कृदन्त adjective, "body-born") locates the guṇas precisely as 14.5 did: they are body-arising, hence the body-bearer can in principle stand apart from them.

14.20.title: अमृतम् अश्नुते — the negative-defined fruit
14.20.body: जन्ममृत्युजरादुःखैः (तृतीया-बहुवचन of a long द्वन्द्व) names what is escaped — birth, death, old age, sorrow. विमुक्तः (कृदन्त past participle, "released") agrees with देही. The verb अश्नुते (आत्मनेपद, "attains") takes अमृतम् as द्वितीया. Liberation is defined as freedom-from before it is described positively.

14.21.title: कैः लिङ्गैः — Arjuna's three-fold diagnostic question
14.21.body: Arjuna asks three things in sequence: कैः लिङ्गैः ("by what signs?"), किमाचारः ("what is his conduct?"), कथम् ... अतिवर्तते ("how does he transcend?"). The तृतीया-बहुवचन of लिङ्गैः is precise — he wants observable marks. प्रभो is सम्बोधन, never in the action. भवति (लट्) and अतिवर्तते (लट्, आत्मनेपद) are the two finite verbs.

14.21.title: Question structure mirroring 14.11–13
14.21.body: Arjuna's question echoes the diagnostic frame Krishna built in 14.11–13: there, signs of each guṇa's dominance; here, signs of all-guṇa transcendence. The verse functions as a doorway — Krishna's reply in 14.22–25 will mirror the question's tripartite structure: marks, conduct, mechanism.

14.22.title: न द्वेष्टि न काङ्क्षति — the symmetric non-reaction
14.22.body: Two finite verbs in tight symmetry: द्वेष्टि and काङ्क्षति, both लट्, both प्रथम-एकवचन, both negated. The objects pair off: सम्प्रवृत्तानि (कृदन्त past participle, "things that have arisen") and निवृत्तानि ("things that have ceased"), each as द्वितीया-बहुवचन. Prakāśa (sattva), pravṛtti (rajas), moha (tamas) are taken as a triad — the guṇātīta neither pushes them away when present nor pulls them back when gone.

14.22.title: The first mark — neutrality toward the three guṇa-signatures
14.22.body: Krishna answers Arjuna's लिङ्ग-question first. The decisive sign is not the absence of prakāśa/pravṛtti/moha — those keep arising and ceasing in any embodied being — but the *non-reactivity* toward them. The guṇātīta is recognised by what he does not do: he does not lean in or away.

14.23.title: उदासीनवत् — the participial simile
14.23.body: उदासीनवत् ("as if indifferent") is the तद्धित suffix -वत् giving comparative force, while आसीनः (शानच् present participle of √आस्, आत्मनेपद, "seated") is the predicate adjective on the implicit subject. गुणैः (तृतीया-बहुवचन) is the instrument by which he is not विचाल्यते (passive of causative वि + √चल् + णिच्, लट्, "is shaken"). The double negation न विचाल्यते / न इङ्गते frames the stability.

14.23.title: गुणाः वर्तन्ते — naming the action's true agent
14.23.body: गुणाः वर्तन्ते ("the guṇas are operating") is what the guṇātīta cognizes — and that cognition itself is what stabilises him. The इत्येवम् marks the proposition as recited inwardly. He stands अवतिष्ठति (लट्, परस्मैपद, "remains established") because he has correctly assigned agency: to the guṇas, not to himself. Theory becomes posture.

14.24.title: समदुःखसुखः — the bahuvrīhi catalogue
14.24.body: Multiple बहुव्रीहि compounds describe the guṇātīta: समदुःखसुखः ("one for whom pain and pleasure are equal"), स्वस्थः ("established in himself"), समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः ("one for whom clod, stone, gold are equal"), तुल्यप्रियाप्रियः ("equal toward pleasant and unpleasant"), capped by धीरः ("steady"). Each compound encodes a domain — feeling, matter, valuation — where the binary collapses into sama.

14.24.title: तुल्यनिन्दात्मसंस्तुतिः — even self-praise neutralised
14.24.body: The final बहुव्रीहि — तुल्यनिन्दात्मसंस्तुतिः ("one for whom blame and self-praise are equal") — is the sharpest. आत्मसंस्तुतिः is self-praise specifically, the hardest evaluation to neutralise. The verse stacks five bahuvrīhis without a finite verb — the cataloguing density itself enacts the equanimity: nothing predicated, only states described.

14.25.title: सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी — the renouncer of all initiatives
14.25.body: सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी is an agent-noun (णिनि suffix, -इन्) तत्पुरुष — "renouncer of all initiatives." Recall 14.12: आरम्भः was rajas's signature. The guṇātīta has dropped not action itself but the rajasic *launching* impulse. मानापमानयोः, मित्रारिपक्षयोः — both सप्तमी dvandvas — name the pairs across which equanimity holds.

14.25.title: गुणातीतः स उच्यते — the chapter's final tag
14.25.body: उच्यते (passive of √वच्, लट्) — "he is called" — formally names the figure: गुणातीतः. The verse concludes the four-verse portrait (14.22–25) with a definitional close. Arjuna's three questions are now answered: marks (22), conduct (23–25 first half), mechanism (23 — "the guṇas are merely operating"). The next two verses will name the path.

14.26.title: अव्यभिचारेण भक्तियोगेन — the instrumental path
14.26.body: अव्यभिचारेण (तृतीया, "unwavering") qualifies भक्तियोगेन (तृतीया, "by the yoga of devotion") — a double instrumental naming the means. सेवते (आत्मनेपद, लट्) takes माम् as द्वितीया. The relative-correlative यः ... सः binds the devotee to the result: समतीत्य (absolutive, "having transcended") गुणान् एतान्, he ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते.

14.26.title: ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते — bhakti as the guṇātīta shortcut
14.26.body: ब्रह्मभूयाय is चतुर्थी (dative of purpose, "for the state of becoming Brahman"); कल्पते (आत्मनेपद, लट्, "is fit") takes the dative. The verse's force: the jñāna-route of 14.19–25 is real but slow; bhakti achieves the same guṇātīta condition. The 22–25 description and the 26 mechanism are the same destination through different doors.

14.27.title: ब्रह्मणः प्रतिष्ठा — Krishna as Brahman's foundation
14.27.body: अहम् is the subject, प्रतिष्ठा ("the support, the ground") the predicate nominative, and four षष्ठी genitives qualify what is grounded: ब्रह्मणः (Brahman), अमृतस्य अव्ययस्य (the imperishable immortal), शाश्वतस्य धर्मस्य (eternal dharma), and ऐकान्तिकस्य सुखस्य (absolute bliss). हि ("for") makes this the warrant for 14.26's promise.

14.27.title: The chapter's structural close — guṇātīta resolved in Krishna
14.27.body: The four genitive abstracts — Brahman, amṛta, dharma, sukha — are precisely what 14.20 had named negatively (freedom from janma/mṛtyu/jarā/duḥkha). Now they are positively located: all four rest on Krishna. The chapter that opened with परं ज्ञानम् (14.1) closes by naming where that knowledge terminates — not in a state but in a Person.
